title: Rewrite OAuth 2.0 description with hook, intuition, and hotel-key-card analogy
description: Current grade: F (9/36). Opens with jargon 'Authorization Code + PKCE and Client Credentials flows.' Rewrite with: HOOK: 'You want to let Spotify read your Facebook friends list. Do you give Spotify your Facebook password?' INTUITION: Hotel key card analogy — temporary, scoped, revocable. Add 'Real-World: Google Sign-In, GitHub OAuth, Sign In with Apple.' Add SUMMARY: 3 bullet takeaway. See audit rewrite section for exact content.
acceptanceCriteria: Topic description opens with a problem-first hook (not definition-first)
Hotel key card analogy appears before any protocol details
Real-world section names Google, GitHub, Apple as OAuth users
3-bullet summary at bottom of properties panel

title: Add 'why' explanations to OAuth step descriptions (currently protocol-formal)
description: Current T4 score: 2/3. Steps read like RFC documentation. Rewrite with WHY: Step 1 should explain WHY PKCE exists ('like tearing a dollar bill in half'). Step 4 should explain WHY the code is useless to an eavesdropper. Step 7 should explain WHY puzzle-piece matching proves identity. See audit for exact rewritten steps.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 5 of 9 PKCE steps have 'why this matters' explanation
PKCE dollar-bill analogy used in step descriptions
Code exchange step explains the security benefit of code_verifier

title: Rewrite CORS description with bouncer analogy and 'localhost:3000' hook
description: Current grade: F (8/36). Never explains WHY CORS exists or what Same-Origin Policy is. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'React app on localhost:3000 calls API on api.example.com — CORS error. Sound familiar?' INTUITION: Bouncer at nightclub analogy. Explain Same-Origin Policy FIRST, then CORS as controlled relaxation. See audit for exact content.
acceptanceCriteria: Topic description mentions the React localhost CORS error experience
Same-Origin Policy explained before CORS
Bouncer analogy used for CORS concept
Real-world note: '#1 most-googled developer error'

title: Complete rewrite of AES-128 content — hook, shredding analogy, 'why' steps
description: Current grade: F (5/36). WORST teaching in the module. Opens with jargon, no intuition, no why, no real-world context. COMPLETE REWRITE: HOOK: 'AES is protecting your bank password right now.' INTUITION: 10 rounds of shred/rearrange/mix/dye. STEPS: explain SubBytes as pattern destruction, ShiftRows as spreading, MixColumns as blending, AddRoundKey as key mixing. REAL-WORLD: iPhone, WhatsApp, AWS S3, WPA3. See audit for exact content.
acceptanceCriteria: Opening description mentions real-world AES usage (phone, WiFi, bank)
Shredding/rearranging/mixing/dyeing analogy in description
Each sub-step description explains WHY it exists, not just what it does
SubBytes explains 'destroys patterns', ShiftRows explains 'spreads influence', MixColumns explains 'blends columns'
Summary: '10 rounds of Substitute + Shift + Mix + XOR-with-key'

title: Rewrite JWT Lifecycle description with sealed-envelope analogy
description: Current grade: F (7/36). No analogy, no hook. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'Every API you use sends invisible tokens with every request.' INTUITION: 'JWT is like a sealed envelope — anyone can read the address (payload), but only the sender can create a valid seal (signature).' Add JWT vs Session Cookies note (top confusion point). Add real-world: Auth0, Firebase, AWS Cognito.
acceptanceCriteria: Sealed envelope analogy in description
Clear statement that JWT payload is NOT encrypted
Real-world services named
'JWT vs Session Cookies' note for the #1 student confusion

title: Rewrite Certificate Chain description with chain-of-trust analogy
description: Current grade: F (9/36). Opens with 'X.509 certificate chain verification' — maximum jargon. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'How does your browser know that google.com is really Google and not an imposter?' INTUITION: 'Certificates are a chain of vouching — your computer trusts a few Root CAs, who vouch for Intermediate CAs, who vouch for the specific website. Like a recommendation letter: you trust your professor, who trusts the hiring manager, who vouches for the candidate.' Add Let's Encrypt context.
acceptanceCriteria: Opens with 'how does your browser know google.com is really Google?'
Chain-of-trust analogy: recommendation letters or vouching chain
Let's Encrypt mentioned as real-world example
Expired/Revoked scenarios explained with why they matter

title: Rewrite Rate Limiting description with bucket/faucet physical analogy
description: Current grade: F (5/36). No analogy despite names BEING analogies. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'Your API gets 10,000 requests per second from a single IP. Is it a popular user or an attacker? Rate limiting decides.' INTUITION: 'Token Bucket: imagine a bucket that refills with tokens. Each request costs one token. When the bucket is empty, requests are rejected until it refills. Leaky Bucket: imagine a bucket with a hole — water (requests) drips in from the top and leaks out at a constant rate.' Add 'when to use which' comparison.
acceptanceCriteria: Physical bucket/faucet analogy for each algorithm
Hook mentions API abuse scenario
'When to use which' decision guide added
Real-world: GitHub 5000/hr, Twitter API, Cloudflare

title: Add HTTPS masquerade-party analogy and 'why 3-way handshake' explanations
description: Current grade: D (15/36). Good visualization but no analogy and missing WHY. Add: INTUITION: 'TLS handshake is like meeting at a masquerade party — verify identity, agree on secret code, lock the room.' Add WHY for each phase: WHY 3-way handshake (both sides confirm they can send AND receive), WHY TLS needs a separate handshake (HTTP was designed without encryption), WHY DNS is separate (addresses ≠ identities).
acceptanceCriteria: Masquerade party or similar analogy in HTTPS description
Each phase has a 'why this step exists' explanation
Total elapsed timing highlighted with 'DNS dominates' insight

title: Add Diffie-Hellman real-world context and man-in-the-middle warning
description: Current grade: D (15/36). Has world-class intuition (paint analogy = 3/3) but ZERO real-world context and ZERO edge case warnings. Add: REAL-WORLD: 'DH is used in every TLS handshake — billions of times per day. The x25519 curve variant is the default in TLS 1.3.' EDGE CASE: 'Vulnerability: DH alone doesn't prevent man-in-the-middle attacks — Eve could intercept and perform DH with both Alice and Bob separately. That's why TLS uses certificates to authenticate the server.'
acceptanceCriteria: 'Billions of times per day' real-world context added
Man-in-the-middle vulnerability explained
Connection to TLS/HTTPS module made explicit
x25519 mentioned as modern DH variant

title: Add Web Attacks hook with breach statistics and stored/DOM XSS variants
description: Current grade: F (11/36). Has excellent T4 (why) but weak everything else. Add: HOOK: 'These three vulnerabilities caused the British Airways breach (380K credit cards), the MySpace worm (1M profiles in 20 hours), and the TalkTalk hack (£60M fine).' Add stored XSS and DOM-based XSS variants. Add 'which defense stops which attack?' comparison table.
acceptanceCriteria: Opening mentions real breach examples with numbers
Stored XSS variant added (separate from reflected)
DOM-based XSS variant added
Defense comparison table showing which defense stops which attack

title: Add 'predict before you click' self-test prompts to all step-through topics
description: T11 (Self-Test) scored 0/3 across ALL 13 topics. Add prediction prompts before key steps: DH: 'Before clicking Step, predict: what will Alice's public key be? (g^a mod p)' CORS: 'Will this configuration pass or fail? Make your prediction before scrolling.' AES: 'After SubBytes, will any byte remain unchanged? Think about it.' This converts passive watching into active learning (Bloom's Level 3: Apply).
acceptanceCriteria: At least 5 topics have at least 1 prediction prompt before a key step
Prompt appears in a visually distinct 'Predict' callout box
Answer is revealed only after user advances to next step
DH, CORS, AES, OAuth, Web Attacks have prediction prompts

title: Add memorable 3-bullet summary to ALL topics (T12 systemic gap)
description: T12 (Summary) scored 0/3 on 10 of 13 topics. Add a persistent 'Key Takeaways' box at the bottom of each canvas with 3 bullets. Examples: OAuth: '(1) OAuth = access without passwords (2) Tokens are temporary and revocable (3) PKCE prevents code interception'. DH: '(1) Shared secret from public exchange (2) Paint mixing = easy to combine, impossible to separate (3) Foundation of TLS key exchange'. These become flash-card-ready content.
acceptanceCriteria: All 13 topics have a 'Key Takeaways' section with 3 bullets
Bullets are concise enough for a flashcard (< 15 words each)
Bullets capture the INSIGHT, not the mechanics

title: Rewrite DDoS description with traffic-jam analogy and mitigation strategies
description: Current grade: F (5/36). Just a widget with no educational context. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'In 2023, Cloudflare blocked 71 million requests per second from a DDoS attack. How do you defend against an army of bots?' INTUITION: 'DDoS is like a traffic jam — thousands of fake cars clogging the highway so real drivers can't get through. Mitigation = building a checkpoint that filters fake cars before they reach the highway.' Add mitigation strategies: CDN, rate limiting, anycast, scrubbing centers.
acceptanceCriteria: Traffic-jam analogy in description
Real-world Cloudflare/AWS Shield statistics
At least 3 mitigation strategies explained (CDN, anycast, scrubbing)
Connection to rate limiting module made

title: Add Password Hashing 'don't use MD5/SHA-256' common mistakes section
description: Current grade: D (13/36). T8 (Mistakes) = 0/3. Add: 'Common Mistake #1: Using MD5 or SHA-256 for passwords — these are FAST hashes, designed for speed. Attackers can try billions of MD5 hashes per second. bcrypt is intentionally SLOW — that's the point.' 'Common Mistake #2: Not using a unique salt per password — two users with password123 get the same hash, and cracking one cracks both.' 'Common Mistake #3: Cost factor too low (< 10) — a cost of 4 takes microseconds to crack.'
acceptanceCriteria: At least 3 common mistakes listed in properties panel or description
Each mistake explains WHY it's dangerous with concrete numbers
'MD5 = billions per second, bcrypt = 10 per second' comparison

title: Improve Encryption Comparison to world-class (current C → target A)
description: Current grade: C (18/36) — best in module but still not world-class. Missing: T1 Hook (1/3), T6 Edge Cases (0/3), T8 Mistakes (0/3). Add: HOOK: 'Every time you buy something online, all three of these encryption methods work together in < 100ms.' EDGE CASE: 'What about quantum computers? RSA breaks, AES survives (with double key length), Hybrid needs post-quantum key exchange.' MISTAKES: 'Don't encrypt large files with RSA — it's 1000x slower than AES. That's why Hybrid exists.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook mentions online shopping and all three working together
Quantum computing edge case mentioned
'Don't encrypt large files with RSA' mistake highlighted
Score improves from 18 to 25+ on re-evaluation
